title: Personalize Agents with Custom Variables & Templates
lede: Turn a generic script into a personal conversation. Feed your Smart Agent live context — the caller's name, address, gate code, anything — so it greets every customer like it already knows them, and save reusable prompt templates so you never rebuild an agent from scratch.

steps.title: Open the Agent You Want to Personalize
steps.content:
Variables and templates live inside each agent's editor, so start by opening one:

  1. In the left sidebar, click Intelligence Center → Smart Agents.
  2. Click the name of the agent you want to personalize. The agent editor opens with the greeting and system prompt in the left column.
💡 No agent yet? Create one first with the Create Your First Agent guide. Everything in this guide works the same for every agent you build.

images.alt: Smart Agents list under Intelligence Center
images.caption: Intelligence Center → Smart Agents — click an agent's name to open its editor.

images.alt: Smart Agents list under Intelligence Center
images.caption: Intelligence Center → Smart Agents — click an agent's name to open its editor.

steps.title: Insert a Variable into the Prompt or Greeting
steps.content:
Any text your agent speaks or follows can contain variables in curly braces — placeholders that are swapped for the caller's real data the moment a call starts.

  1. Click into the system prompt (the big text box) or the first message field at the top.
  2. Type an opening brace { — a variable menu pops up instantly. You can also click the {...} button in the top-right corner of the field.
  3. Pick a variable to insert it at your cursor. The core CRM variables are:
    • {first_name} — the caller's first name
    • {last_name} — the caller's last name
    • {email} — the caller's email address
    • {full_address} — the complete address on file

Example greeting: "Hi {first_name}! Thanks for calling — are you still at {full_address}?" On a real call this becomes "Hi Sarah! Thanks for calling — are you still at 12 Ocean Drive, Miami…"

💡 Variables work in the greeting, the system prompt, sub-agent and task prompts, and even the SMS / WhatsApp / Email channel prompts — anywhere the {...} button appears.

images.alt: Agent editor with the first message field and system prompt
images.caption: The agent editor — the first message field sits on top, the system prompt below; both accept variables.

images.alt: Agent editor with the first message field and system prompt
images.caption: The agent editor — the first message field sits on top, the system prompt below; both accept variables.

images.alt: Variable autocomplete menu open over the system prompt
images.caption: Type { in any prompt field (or click the {...} button) to open the variable menu.

images.alt: Variable autocomplete menu open over the system prompt
images.caption: Type { in any prompt field (or click the {...} button) to open the variable menu.

steps.title: Know the Full Variable Catalog
steps.content:
Beyond the four in the menu, iMash resolves many more placeholders at call time — type them by hand anywhere in a prompt:

  • Address parts: {street_address}, {apartment_suite}, {city}, {state}, {zipcode}, {country} — when you need just one piece instead of {full_address}.
  • Tags: {tag_1}, {tag_2}, … — the tags on the matched lead or contact, in order.
  • Custom Data fields: {custom_vars_<field>} — any field you add to a lead or contact's Custom Data tab (next step). A field named gate_code becomes {custom_vars_gate_code}.
  • Call & time system variables: {system_from} (caller's number), {system_to} (number dialed), {system_direction} (inbound/outbound), {system_call_type} (Phone or Web), and {system_time_UTC} or any timezone like {system_time_America_New_York} — perfect for "our current local time is…" logic.
⚠️ A variable with no value is silently removed from the text at call time. Phrase sentences so they still read naturally if the data is missing — "Hi {first_name}, thanks for calling!" degrades gracefully; "Your name is {first_name}." does not.

steps.title: Feed Variables from a Lead or Contact's Custom Data
steps.content:
Where do the values come from? When a call starts, iMash matches the phone number to your CRM and loads that person's record — name, address, tags, and every field on their Custom Data tab. To add your own fields:

  1. In the left sidebar, click CRM → Leads (or Contacts) and open a record.
  2. Click the Custom Data tab.
  3. Click + Add Field, enter a Field Name (e.g. gate_code) and a Value (e.g. 4482), then click Save.
  4. Use Edit to change a value or the trash icon to remove a field.

From now on, whenever your agent talks to this person, {custom_vars_gate_code} resolves to 4482 — so the agent can tell the technician the gate code, confirm the preferred technician, or reference anything else you store.

images.alt: Lead details with the Custom Data tab showing custom fields
images.caption: The Custom Data tab on a lead — each field becomes a {custom_vars_...} variable for the agent.

images.alt: Lead details with the Custom Data tab showing custom fields
images.caption: The Custom Data tab on a lead — each field becomes a {custom_vars_...} variable for the agent.

steps.title: Send Variables from Campaigns or the API
steps.content:
CRM records aren't the only source. Values can arrive with the call itself:

  • Campaigns & dial lists — when a campaign dials a lead or contact from your list, that record's fields and Custom Data are loaded automatically, so {first_name} and friends personalize every outbound call with zero extra setup.
  • API calls — when you start a call through the iMash API, include a custom_vars object with your own key/value pairs in the request. Those values override or extend the CRM data for that one call — ideal for passing context from an external CRM, booking system, or app.
💡 Priority order: values sent via the API win over the CRM record's values, and both are topped up with the built-in system variables. See the API Reference for the exact request format.

steps.title: Open Templates & Variables
steps.content:
Now for the power feature: reusable prompt templates with your own custom variables. In the agent editor:

  1. Look at the panel on the right — the list that starts with Knowledge Base.
  2. Click the last item, Templates & Variables. (In the node-based editor, the same item appears as Templates in the top capabilities bar.)
  3. The Templates & Variables window opens with two sections in its left rail: Variables and Templates.
💡 The number badge next to the item shows how many custom variables this agent already has defined.

images.alt: Agent editor right panel with Templates & Variables at the bottom
images.caption: Templates & Variables is the last item in the agent editor's right-hand panel.

images.alt: Agent editor right panel with Templates & Variables at the bottom
images.caption: Templates & Variables is the last item in the agent editor's right-hand panel.

steps.title: Create Reusable [[Variables]]
steps.content:
These are your own named values, written with double square brackets — [[variable_name]]. Define them once, reuse them across every template: your company name, business hours, service area, promo of the month.

  1. In the Variables section, type a Variable Name (e.g. company_name) and a Value (e.g. Acme Home Services).
  2. Click Add. The variable appears in the list as [[company_name]].
  3. Edit a value any time by typing directly in its box; remove one with the red trash icon.
  4. Click Done when finished.
💡 The difference in one line: [[double_brackets]] are filled in when you load a template (rebranding a prompt in one click), while {curly_braces} are filled in live on each call with the caller's data. Use both together for maximum leverage.

images.alt: Variables section of the Templates & Variables window
images.caption: Add reusable [[variables]] — a name and a value — then use them inside any template.

images.alt: Variables section of the Templates & Variables window
images.caption: Add reusable [[variables]] — a name and a value — then use them inside any template.

steps.title: Build a Prompt Template
steps.content:
A template is a saved prompt (or a set of prompts) you can load into any agent whenever you need it.

  1. Click Templates in the left rail of the window.
  2. Click + Create New Template.
  3. Give it a clear Template Name (e.g. Standard Greeting).
  4. Write the prompt content in the Main Prompt tab. Mix both variable styles freely: [[company_name]] for values you defined in the Variables section, and {first_name} for live caller data (the {...} button works here too).
  5. Need a multi-part template? Click + Add Prompt to add more prompt tabs; double-click a tab's name to rename it, or hit the small ✕ to remove it.
  6. Click Save Template.

images.alt: New template editor with name, prompt tabs and content
images.caption: The template editor — name it, write the content with [[variables]] and {variables}, then Save Template.

images.alt: New template editor with name, prompt tabs and content
images.caption: The template editor — name it, write the content with [[variables]] and {variables}, then Save Template.

steps.title: Load a Template into Your Agent
steps.content:
Saved templates are shared across your whole account — build once, use in every agent. Each template row offers:

  • Load — replaces the agent's system prompt with the template content. Every [[variable]] is substituted with its current value from the Variables section at that moment.
  • Auto-sync switch — binds this agent to the template, so it stays linked as the template's "active" source. Flip it off to unbind.
  • Pencil icon — edit the template's name, prompts, or content.
  • Trash icon — delete the template (a confirmation is asked first).
⚠️ Load overwrites the agent's current system prompt. If you've hand-tuned the prompt, copy it somewhere safe (or save it as its own template) before loading a different one.

images.alt: Templates list with Load, auto-sync, edit and delete actions
images.caption: Each saved template can be loaded into the agent, auto-synced, edited, or deleted.

images.alt: Templates list with Load, auto-sync, edit and delete actions
images.caption: Each saved template can be loaded into the agent, auto-synced, edited, or deleted.

steps.title: Test the Personalization
steps.content:
Time for the payoff — hear your agent greet someone by name:

  1. In the agent editor, click Test Agent (top-right) or Connect in the chat panel on the right.
  2. Better yet, call the agent from a phone number that exists on one of your leads or contacts — the CRM match happens automatically and every variable fills in with that person's real data.
  3. Check the transcript afterwards in Call Logs to confirm the variables resolved the way you expected.

That's it — one prompt, infinite personal conversations. Your agent now sounds like it prepared for every single call, because it did.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference
  • 🎥 Watch our video tutorials
